abstract: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) represents a major global health challenge, characterized by progressive airway obstruction and significant systemic effects that contribute to high morbidity and mortality. While the clinical focus is often on respiratory function, comorbid mental health disorders—specifically depression and anxiety—frequently coexist with COPD, affecting approximately half of the patient population. These psychological factors are often overlooked in clinical settings despite their profound impact on patient quality of life and their tendency to exacerbate the physical symptoms of the disease. This review examines the high prevalence of depressive symptoms among COPD patients and highlights the necessity of integrating psychological assessment into routine care. Furthermore, the paper provides a comprehensive overview of the various diagnostic tools and standardized questionnaires currently utilized to screen for and evaluate the severity of depression in this vulnerable population.
